content.text: Most AWS regions are enabled by default on every account. A growing subset of newer regions — particularly those in Asia Pacific, Europe, the Middle East, and Africa — require explicit opt-in before you can provision resources in them. This opt-in requirement exists to protect accounts from unexpected charges and to satisfy local regulatory requirements about data sovereignty.

attrs.alt: AWS regions split into default-enabled and opt-in-required categories, with the enablement flow diagram
attrs.title: AWS Opt-In Regions Overview

content.text: Older AWS regions (launched before 2019) were enabled by default because customers had already built workflows that assumed automatic access. When AWS began launching regions in countries with stricter data-sovereignty requirements — such as Hong Kong (subject to China's NSL), Bahrain (GCC sovereign cloud expectations), and the UAE (UAE PDPL) — it introduced opt-in to give account owners deliberate control over where their data could flow.
— prevents accidental resource creation in regions you don't intend to use, avoiding surprise costs.
— opting in is a deliberate acknowledgment that you accept the legal and compliance implications of operating in that jurisdiction.

— SCPs and permission boundaries in new regions don't apply automatically if the region is never enabled; opt-in ensures your governance policies extend to that region before any resource can be created.
Subject to HK NSL; data accessible to mainland China under certain conditions
PDPL (Bahrain); suitable for Gulf region data residency
POPIA compliance; first AWS region in Africa
GDPR-compliant; supports Italian public sector workloads
Indonesia PDPA; first dedicated Indonesia region
DPDP Act; second India region for DR pairing with ap-south-1
Swiss FADP; Switzerland is not in EU, separate data residency requirement
GDPR-compliant; ENS High certification for Spanish public sector
UAE PDPL; first dedicated UAE region
Australian Privacy Act; DR pair for ap-southeast-2 (Sydney)
Israeli Privacy Protection Law; first dedicated Israel region

You enable opt-in regions at the account level. In an AWS Organizations setup, the management account controls opt-in status for member accounts — a member account cannot opt itself into a region unless the organization allows it.

# Enable an opt-in region for the current account
aws account enable-region \
  --region-name ap-southeast-3

# Check the status of an opt-in region
aws account get-region-opt-status \
  --region-name ap-southeast-3
# Output: {"RegionName": "ap-southeast-3", "RegionOptStatus": "ENABLED"}
# Possible values: ENABLED | ENABLING | DISABLED | DISABLING | ENABLED_BY_DEFAULT

# List all regions with their opt-in status
aws ec2 describe-regions \
  --all-regions \
  --query 'sort_by(Regions, &RegionName)[*].{Region:RegionName,Status:OptInStatus}' \
  --output table

# Disable a previously enabled opt-in region
# WARNING: all resources in that region must be deleted first
aws account disable-region \
  --region-name ap-southeast-3
CLI subcommand targets the AWS Account Management API, which is a global service. It does NOT need a
flag — the command operates on the account itself, not on a regional endpoint.
In an AWS Organizations setup, opt-in region enablement works differently depending on whether you're in the management account or a member account:
Member account (region access controlled by Org)
Control Tower governance; enable via CT Account Factory

# Enable a region for a specific member account (from management account)
aws account enable-region \
  --region-name il-central-1 \
  --account-id 111122223333

# Enable a region for ALL accounts in an OU (requires Organizations policy)
# This is typically done via Control Tower or a custom Lambda

# Check opt-in status for a member account
aws account get-region-opt-status \
  --region-name il-central-1 \
  --account-id 111122223333

# List all member accounts that have a specific region enabled
# (useful audit query via Organizations + account API)
aws organizations list-accounts \
  --query 'Accounts[*].Id' \
  --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read account_id; do
    STATUS=$(aws account get-region-opt-status \
      --region-name me-central-1 \
      --account-id "$account_id" \
      --query 'RegionOptStatus' \
      --output text 2>/dev/null)
    echo "$account_id: $STATUS"
  done
Enabling an opt-in region is not instantaneous. AWS provisions the region endpoints and IAM infrastructure for your account. The full sequence takes 5–15 minutes:
. Basic EC2 and IAM APIs become available.
Regional services propagate over the next 10–20 minutes. Some services may respond with errors until fully initialized.
Your existing SCPs apply immediately once the region is enabled — no need to re-push policies.

# Poll until a region is fully enabled
REGION="me-central-1"
while true; do
  STATUS=$(aws account get-region-opt-status \
    --region-name "$REGION" \
    --query 'RegionOptStatus' \
    --output text)
  echo "$(date '+%H:%M:%S') $REGION: $STATUS"
  if [ "$STATUS" = "ENABLED" ]; then
    echo "Region is ready"
    break
  fi
  sleep 30
done

# Verify basic access once ENABLED
aws ec2 describe-availability-zones \
  --region "$REGION" \
  --query 'AvailabilityZones[*].ZoneName'
Your existing Organization SCPs automatically apply to opt-in regions once they are enabled. However, you should verify that your baseline security controls are in place before deploying workloads:

# After enabling me-central-1, verify Security Hub is available and enable it
aws securityhub enable-security-hub \
  --region me-central-1 \
  --enable-default-standards

# Enable CloudTrail logging for the new region
# (if using an organization trail, it automatically covers new regions)
aws cloudtrail get-trail-status \
  --name arn:aws:cloudtrail:us-east-1:123456789012:trail/org-trail \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --query 'IsLogging'

# Check GuardDuty auto-enable in Organizations
aws guardduty list-detectors \
  --region me-central-1
# If empty, GuardDuty needs to be enabled for this region

aws guardduty create-detector \
  --enable \
  --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES \
  --region me-central-1

If you use AWS Control Tower, it does NOT automatically govern newly enabled opt-in regions. You must explicitly register the region in Control Tower to apply guardrails and account baseline. In the Control Tower console, navigate to Landing zone settings → Governed Regions and add the region.
Region not yet opted in; IAM can't authorize requests to unregistered regions
Enable from management account or update SCP to allow the action
Propagation takes 10–20 min; some services lag behind EC2/IAM
Wait and retry; check Service Health Dashboard for that region
Security services don't auto-extend to newly opted-in regions in all cases
Explicitly enable each security service in the new region or use delegated auto-enable
CT doesn't auto-govern opt-in regions even after enablement
Register region in CT Landing Zone settings → Governed Regions
You can disable a previously opted-in region, but only after deleting all resources in it. AWS will refuse the disable request if any resources remain — this protects against accidental data loss.

# Check for remaining resources before disabling
# (No single API covers everything — check the most common services)
for SERVICE in ec2 rds s3api elasticloadbalancing lambda; do
  echo "=== $SERVICE ==="
  case $SERVICE in
    ec2) aws ec2 describe-instances --region ap-east-1 \
           --query 'Reservations[*].Instances[*].InstanceId' \
           --output text ;;
    rds) aws rds describe-db-instances --region ap-east-1 \
           --query 'DBInstances[*].DBInstanceIdentifier' \
           --output text ;;
    s3api) aws s3api list-buckets \
           --query 'Buckets[*].Name' \
           --output text ;;
    lambda) aws lambda list-functions --region ap-east-1 \
           --query 'Functions[*].FunctionName' \
           --output text ;;
  esac
done

# After confirming no resources remain:
aws account disable-region \
  --region-name ap-east-1
Automating Opt-In Region Enablement Across the Organization

When onboarding a new opt-in region for a multi-account organization, enabling it one account at a time is impractical. Use a Lambda function triggered by account creation events, or run an Organizations-level script, to bulk-enable the region:

import boto3
import time

ORGANIZATIONS_REGION = "us-east-1"  # Orgs API is global; use us-east-1
TARGET_REGION = "me-central-1"       # Region to enable across all accounts

def enable_region_for_all_accounts(target_region: str) -> None:
    orgs = boto3.client("organizations", region_name=ORGANIZATIONS_REGION)

    # Page through all accounts in the organization
    paginator = orgs.get_paginator("list_accounts")
    accounts = []
    for page in paginator.paginate():
        accounts.extend(page["Accounts"])

    print(f"Found {len(accounts)} accounts. Enabling {target_region}...")

    for account in accounts:
        account_id = account["Id"]
        account_name = account["Name"]

        if account["Status"] != "ACTIVE":
            print(f"  SKIP {account_id} ({account_name}) — not active")
            continue

        try:
            acct_client = boto3.client(
                "account",
                region_name=ORGANIZATIONS_REGION
            )
            acct_client.enable_region(
                AccountId=account_id,
                RegionName=target_region
            )
            print(f"  ENABLED {account_id} ({account_name})")
        except acct_client.exceptions.ConflictException:
            print(f"  ALREADY ENABLED {account_id} ({account_name})")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"  ERROR {account_id} ({account_name}): {e}")

        time.sleep(0.1)  # Basic rate-limit protection

if __name__ == "__main__":
    enable_region_for_all_accounts(TARGET_REGION)
Auto-Enable via EventBridge on Account Creation
In a growing organization, new accounts are created regularly via Account Factory. You can auto-enable your approved opt-in regions on every new account by reacting to the
Route this EventBridge rule to a Lambda that calls
for each approved opt-in region. New accounts will have the regions enabled within minutes of creation.

Not every AWS service is available in opt-in regions, particularly the newest ones. Opt-in regions follow the same launch-wave pattern as other regions, but they often start with a smaller footprint and expand over months. Always verify service availability before designing an architecture around an opt-in region.
GPU / HPC instances (p4d, p5, hpc6a), Trainium, Inferentia
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval (varies by region)
IAM, CloudTrail, Config, GuardDuty, Security Hub
VPC, ALB, NLB, Direct Connect, Transit Gateway

# Check which services are available in a specific opt-in region
aws ssm get-parameters-by-path \
  --path "/aws/service/global-infrastructure/regions/me-central-1/services" \
  --recursive \
  --query 'Parameters[*].Name' \
  --output text \
  --region us-east-1 | tr '\t' '\n' | \
  sed 's|.*/regions/me-central-1/services/||' | sort

# Compare with a mature region to see what's missing
for SVC in bedrock vpc-lattice securitylake macie2; do
  STATUS=$(aws ssm get-parameter \
    --name "/aws/service/global-infrastructure/services/${SVC}/regions/me-central-1" \
    --region us-east-1 \
    --query 'Parameter.Value' \
    --output text 2>/dev/null && echo "available" || echo "NOT available")
  echo "$SVC in me-central-1: $STATUS"
done
A newly enabled opt-in region is a blank slate — no security services are active by default. Before deploying any workload, establish a baseline security posture using this checklist:
— verify your organization trail covers the new region (it does if it's a multi-region trail). Confirm with
— enable the Config recorder to track resource configurations. Organization recorders extend automatically, but verify with
— enable a detector in the new region. If you have organization auto-enable configured, check whether it applies to opt-in regions (it may not in all configurations).
— enable with default standards (AWS Foundational Best Practices, CIS) to get immediate visibility into misconfigurations.
— the default VPC in a new region has the same permissive defaults as any region (public subnets, open NACL). Delete the default VPC and use your standard VPC template from day one.

NEW_REGION="me-central-1"

# 1. Verify org trail covers the new region
aws cloudtrail describe-trails \
  --region "$NEW_REGION" \
  --include-shadow-trails \
  --query 'trailList[?IsOrganizationTrail==`true`].{Name:Name,IsLogging:IsLogging}'

# 2. Enable GuardDuty
aws guardduty create-detector \
  --enable \
  --finding-publishing-frequency FIFTEEN_MINUTES \
  --region "$NEW_REGION"

# 3. Enable Security Hub with AWS Foundational Best Practices
aws securityhub enable-security-hub \
  --region "$NEW_REGION" \
  --enable-default-standards

# 4. Delete the default VPC (after confirming no resources are in it)
DEFAULT_VPC=$(aws ec2 describe-vpcs \
  --region "$NEW_REGION" \
  --filters Name=isDefault,Values=true \
  --query 'Vpcs[0].VpcId' \
  --output text)

if [ "$DEFAULT_VPC" != "None" ]; then
  echo "Deleting default VPC $DEFAULT_VPC in $NEW_REGION"
  # (Delete subnets, IGW, then VPC)
  # ... (abbreviated; full deletion requires removing IGW and subnets first)
fi

Opt-in regions tend to have higher prices than mature commercial regions. They have smaller physical footprints (fewer AZs, fewer data centers), which means less economies-of-scale pressure on AWS's infrastructure costs. Prices generally decrease over time as a region matures and capacity grows.

The pricing premium is usually justified by the compliance or latency benefits the opt-in region provides. An Indian fintech under RBI mandate must use ap-south-1 (Mumbai) regardless of price — the alternative is a regulatory violation. Build this premium into your cloud cost projections when planning opt-in region deployments.
Savings Plans and Reserved Instances in Opt-In Regions

Compute Savings Plans apply across all regions including opt-in regions, making them ideal for workloads that span commercial and opt-in regions. Standard Reserved Instances are region-scoped — buy them in the specific opt-in region where your workload runs. Given the 40%+ on-demand premium in some opt-in regions, the discount value of a 1-year RI is even more impactful:

# Find Reserved Instance offerings for an opt-in region
aws ec2 describe-reserved-instances-offerings \
  --region af-south-1 \
  --instance-type m5.xlarge \
  --product-description "Linux/UNIX" \
  --offering-class standard \
  --offering-type "All Upfront" \
  --query 'ReservedInstancesOfferings[?Duration==`31536000`].{Term:Duration,Price:FixedPrice,Savings:UsagePrice}' \
  --output table

# Verify Compute Savings Plans discount in an opt-in region
# (Savings Plans auto-apply; verify utilization via Cost Explorer)
aws ce get-savings-plans-utilization \
  --time-period Start=2024-06-01,End=2024-06-30 \
  --filter '{
    "Dimensions": {
      "Key": "REGION",
      "Values": ["af-south-1"]
    }
  }' \
  --query 'Total.UtilizationPercentage'

Once a region is enabled and workloads are deployed, you need the same observability you have in your primary regions. CloudWatch metrics, alarms, and dashboards are region-scoped — data in me-central-1 does not automatically appear in dashboards configured in us-east-1.

import boto3

# Create a CloudWatch alarm in an opt-in region
cw = boto3.client("cloudwatch", region_name="me-central-1")

cw.put_metric_alarm(
    AlarmName="HighCPU-me-central-1",
    ComparisonOperator="GreaterThanThreshold",
    EvaluationPeriods=2,
    MetricName="CPUUtilization",
    Namespace="AWS/EC2",
    Period=300,
    Statistic="Average",
    Threshold=80.0,
    ActionsEnabled=True,
    AlarmActions=[
        # SNS topic must also exist in the same region
        "arn:aws:sns:me-central-1:123456789012:ops-alerts"
    ],
    Dimensions=[
        {"Name": "AutoScalingGroupName", "Value": "web-asg"}
    ],
    TreatMissingData="notBreaching",
)

# Cross-region metrics can be viewed in CloudWatch cross-account
# observability if you set up sharing between accounts/regions
must exist in the same region as the CloudWatch alarm. Create them in the opt-in region before setting up alerts.
support cross-region metric widgets — you can add a widget from me-central-1 to a dashboard in us-east-1 by specifying the region in the metric widget configuration.
for opt-in regions are delivered to your Health dashboard as-is; no special configuration is needed to receive service health notifications for those regions.

are regional — if your opt-in region runs Lambda or ECS workloads, enable X-Ray in that region separately and use X-Ray cross-account or CloudWatch ServiceLens to correlate with primary region traces.
Opt-in regions require explicit enablement before any resource can be deployed in them — they are not accessible by default.
All newer AWS regions (post-2019) are opt-in; older commercial regions are enabled by default.
In AWS Organizations, only the management account (or a delegated admin) can enable opt-in regions for member accounts.
After opting in, allow 5–20 minutes for the region to become fully operational — poll
Existing SCPs apply immediately, but security services (GuardDuty, Security Hub) and Control Tower governance must be explicitly extended to newly enabled regions.
